Stationery means writing and office material and stationary means in station
Stationery means writing and office material and stationary means immobile
Stationery means standing still and stationary means writing and office material
explanation: Stationery (with -e-) = paper/office supplies; stationary (with -a-) = not moving.

stem: Complaisant and Complacent
options: Complaisant means overconfident and complacent means eager to please
Complaisant means confident and complacent means overconfident
Complaisant means eager to please and complacent means to manifest universally
Complaisant means eager to please and complacent means overconfident
explanation: Complaisant = willing to please/obliging; complacent = smugly self-satisfied.

stem: Enquiry and Inquiry
options: Enquiry means to seek information and inquiry means to investigate
Enquiry means to investigate and inquiry means to seek information
Enquiry means to seek information and inquiry means act of investiture
Enquiry means act of investiture and inquiry means to investigate
explanation: By the common British distinction, ‘enquiry’ = a general request for information; ‘inquiry’ = a formal investigation.

stem: Masterful and Masterly
options: Masterful means imperious and masterly means autocratic
Masterful means skilful and masterly means imperious
Masterful means leader-like and masterly means skilful
Masterful means imperious and masterly means highly skilful
explanation: Masterful = domineering/commanding; masterly = performed with great skill.

stem: Boarder and Border
options: Boarder means a food-and-lodge resident and border means boundary
Boarder means boundary and border means a food-and-lodge resident
Boarder means one who fits planks on the surfaces and border means boundary
Boarder means wider and border means one who fits planks on the surfaces
explanation: A boarder is a lodger who receives meals; a border is a boundary/edge.

stem: Concurrent and Consecutive
options: Concurrent means occurring at the same time and consecutive means occurring one after the other
Concurrent means occurring non-simultaneously and consecutive means occurring one after the other
Concurrent means occurring at the same time and consecutive means occurring thereafter
Concurrent means occurring thereafter and consecutive means occurring at the same time
explanation: Concurrent = simultaneous; consecutive = in unbroken succession.

stem: Epitaph and Epithet
options: Epitaph means a tombstone and epithet means a phrase expressing an attribute
Epitaph means words written on a tombstone and epithet means a phrase expressing an attribute
Epitaph means unspoken words and epithet means a phrase expressing an attribute
Epitaph means words written on a stone and epithet means an award
explanation: Epitaph = an inscription for the dead; epithet = a descriptive term/phrase attached to a name.

stem: The leader's views were equivocal on the contentious issue.
explanation: Equivocal = ambiguous/open to two interpretations; opposite is clear. (Obscure, ambiguous, vague are synonyms.)

stem: The authorities interacted with a group of mutinous students.
explanation: Mutinous = rebellious/refusing to obey; opposite is obedient. (Unruly, contumacious, seditious are synonyms.)

stem: His behaviour was quite belligerent.
explanation: Belligerent = hostile/aggressive; opposite is amiable (friendly). (Bellicose, antagonistic, combative are synonyms.)

stem: He is often accused of being iniquitous in his conduct.
explanation: Iniquitous = grossly unfair/wicked; opposite is virtuous. (Preposterous, execrable, revolting are not opposites.)

stem: A watched pot never boils
options: To experience failure due to over eagerness
To have no chance of achieving something
Desperate anticipation makes time drag on
explanation: Anxiously waiting for something makes it seem to take far longer.

stem: Look to your laurels
options: To make an extra effort to secure one's position
To look for an escape from the current situation
explanation: To be careful not to lose your position to rivals; guard your achievements by continued effort.

stem: Play devil's advocate
options: To try to defeat someone by tricking them
To perform with a lot of skills
To pretend to be against an idea in order to discuss it more carefully
explanation: To argue an opposing view for the sake of debate/testing an argument.

stem: Be in the same boat
options: To say two things in the same breath
To be in the same unpleasant situation
To do something that is dangerous
To pretend to be something that you are not
explanation: To share the same (usually difficult) circumstances as others.

stem: Tilt at windmills
options: To waste time dealing with enemies and problems that do not exist
explanation: From Don Quixote attacking windmills; to fight imaginary opponents.

stem: The actor narrated the story in a resonant voice.
explanation: Resonant (of a voice) = deep, rich and full.

stem: They were fed-up with his doltish behaviour.
explanation: Doltish = stupid/dull-witted; nearest synonym is imbecilic. (Wise, clever, precocious are opposites.)

stem: Anarchism  1. Absence of government and/or authority  2. Chaos and disorder in the absence of government Which of the two suggested meanings is/are correct?
explanation: Anarchism (the political doctrine) = absence of government/authority (meaning 1). ‘Chaos and disorder’ (meaning 2) is the loose, colloquial sense of ‘anarchy’, not of the doctrine.

stem: Pristine  1. In the original condition  2. Extremely polluted / defiled condition Which of the two suggested meanings is/are correct?
explanation: Pristine = in its original, unspoilt condition (1). ‘Extremely polluted/defiled’ (2) is the opposite.

stem: Enormity  1. The fact of something being serious and grave  2. Abominable crime or sin Which of the two suggested meanings is/are correct?
explanation: Enormity = the fact of being extremely serious/grave (1) and also a monstrous crime or sin (2). Both are correct senses; it does NOT mean large size.

stem: Quotidian  1. To quote inaccurately  2. Someone fond of quoting others Which of the two suggested meanings is/are correct?
explanation: Quotidian = daily/ordinary/commonplace. Neither ‘to quote inaccurately’ nor ‘fond of quoting’ is correct.

stem: Immolate  1. To kill somebody or self by burning  2. To kill somebody or self or an animal as an act of sacrifice Which of the two suggested meanings is/are correct?
explanation: Immolate = to kill (oneself/another) by burning (1) and, more broadly, to kill as a sacrifice (2). Both correct.

stem: Minimise  1. Reduce something to the smallest possible degree  2. Reduce expenses, or the degree of anything or of any activity Which of the two suggested meanings is/are correct?
explanation: Minimise = reduce to the smallest possible degree (1) and reduce expenses/the degree of any activity (2). Both are valid senses.

stem: Deify  1. To treat someone in high office with utmost reverence  2. Worship or treat someone as a god Which of the two suggested meanings is/are correct?
explanation: Deify = to worship or treat someone as a god (2). Merely showing reverence to a high office (1) is not deification.

stem: Raconteur  1. Someone who is capable of telling stories in an interesting manner  2. Somebody who believes in only telling tall tales Which of the two suggested meanings is/are correct?
explanation: A raconteur = someone skilled at telling stories in an interesting way (1). It does not imply telling ‘tall tales’ (2).

stem: Emasculate  1. Making someone strong and powerful  2. Increase one's musculature Which of the two suggested meanings is/are correct?
explanation: Emasculate = to weaken/deprive of force or effectiveness — the opposite of ‘making strong’ (1) or ‘increasing musculature’ (2).

stem: The officer under suspension felt intimated by the committee members during his interrogation regarding the allegations against him.
explanation: An officer under suspension who ‘felt ___ by the committee during interrogation’ was frightened/coerced = intimidated. ‘Intimated’ = hinted/announced, which is wrong.

stem: Amongst them all, she was the only one with the perquisite to understand the complexity of the situation.
explanation: Only she had the keen insight to grasp the complexity = perspicacity (sharp discernment). ‘Perquisite’ = a perk/benefit, which doesn’t fit.

stem: At the official function organised for the attending dignitaries, guests arrived in scrumptious attire for the banquet.
explanation: Guests arrived in lavish/splendid attire = sumptuous. ‘Scrumptious’ (delicious) applies to food, not clothes.

stem: The lake atop the mountain has always been the lyre for intrepid hikers.
explanation: The lake has always been the ‘___ for intrepid hikers’ = a lure (an attraction). ‘Lyre’ (a musical instrument) is a sound-alike error.

stem: The candidates went through an intensive instruction process before their absorption into the service.
explanation: Trainees go through instruction before their ‘___ into the service’ = induction (formal entry). ‘Absorption/immersion’ are less precise for joining a service.

stem: The office memorandum directed personnel to minimise their consumption of office stationery by up to twenty percent.
explanation: You ‘reduce consumption by twenty percent’; ‘minimise’ means cut to the least possible, which doesn’t combine with a fixed percentage. (‘Lower’ also works, but ‘reduce’ is the standard correction.)

stem: The announcement of an unexpected bonus led to great tumult among the staff.
explanation: An unexpected bonus is good news, so it produces joy = merriment. ‘Tumult/uproar/upheaval’ suggest disorder, wrong for a happy event.

stem: The scurrilous remarks in the article were met with great joy by the leadership of the company.
explanation: The remarks were ‘met with great joy by the leadership’, so they must be praising = laudatory. ‘Scurrilous’ (abusive) contradicts the joy.

stem: His inappropriate behaviour earned plaudits from his colleagues.
explanation: ‘Inappropriate behaviour’ would earn disgrace/harsh criticism = opprobrium. ‘Plaudits’ (praise) is the wrong direction.

stem: The enormity of the mountain range stunned the visitors to the hill station.
explanation: To describe the vast size of a mountain range use ‘enormousness’. ‘Enormity’ properly means great wickedness, not great size.

stem: Pare, Pair, Pear  1. It is important to pare the branches of a tree to prevent excessive growth.  2. The pair of trees looked identical from a distance.  3. The pear tree was revered by the children of the locality for the bounty it offered each year. In which of the sentences given above has / have the word(s) been used correctly?
explanation: 1 ‘pare the branches’ ✓ (trim); 2 ‘the pair of trees’ ✓ (two); 3 ‘the pear tree … bounty’ ✓ (fruit). All correct.

stem: Seance, Sconce, Scone  1. Those who practise spiritualism are at times seen to participate in a seance.  2. They enjoyed eating the sconce that were served to them in the exclusive club.  3. The scone reading on the graph indicated the correct value output of the experiment. In which of the sentences given above has / have the word(s) been used correctly?
explanation: 1 ‘participate in a seance’ ✓ (spiritualist sitting). 2 ‘eating the sconce’ ✗ (should be ‘scone’, the baked item); 3 ‘scone reading on the graph’ ✗ (neither word denotes a graph reading — a ‘sconce’ is a wall light-bracket).

stem: Truism, Altruism  1. That one has to reap what one sows is a truism.  2. The altruism uttered by the seer covered the higher levels of spiritual knowledge. In which of the sentences given above has / have the word(s) been used correctly?
explanation: 1 ‘reap what one sows is a truism’ ✓ (an obvious truth). 2 ‘the altruism uttered by the seer’ ✗ — altruism = selflessness, not a statement that can be ‘uttered’ (a ‘truism/aphorism’ was meant).

stem: Consequent, Consequence  1. Consequent to the decisions taken by the committee a new set of procedures was brought into force.  2. The past is of little consequence when one is willing to bravely face the challenges of the future. In which of the sentences given above has / have the word(s) been used correctly?
explanation: 1 ‘Consequent to the decisions … procedures were brought in’ ✓ (following as a result); 2 ‘the past is of little consequence’ ✓ (importance). Both correct.

stem: Seize, Cease, Crease  1. The customs department decided to seize the consignment of contraband goods.  2. To improve relations the two parties decided that the hostilities between them should cease.  3. Military personnel are required to be attired in trousers with a knife-edge-like crease. In which of the sentences given above has / have the word(s) been used correctly?
explanation: 1 ‘seize the contraband’ ✓ (confiscate); 2 ‘hostilities should cease’ ✓ (stop); 3 ‘a knife-edge-like crease’ ✓ (fold/line). All correct.

stem: Listen with half an ear
explanation: To listen only partly/inattentively.

stem: Dyed in the wool
options: Changing notions because of a fluffy mind
Unchanging and firm in belief and conviction
explanation: Fixed in one’s views; thoroughgoing (from wool dyed before spinning, so the colour is permanent).

stem: Let sleeping dogs lie
options: Let things remain as they are because of laziness
Not interfere in situations where interference may exacerbate matters
explanation: Leave a settled situation alone to avoid stirring up trouble.

word: A stitch in time saves nine
stem: A stitch in time saves nine
options: Tying up complex matters with clever manoeuvres
Poking at something repeatedly to rescue a situation
Making unlikely connections among disparate subjects
Timely addressal of a problem prevents future exacerbation of the same
explanation: Prompt action prevents a small problem from becoming a big one.

stem: Straight and narrow
options: Honest and morally acceptable way of being
To not deviate from one's goal
explanation: To live honestly and morally (the ‘straight and narrow’ path).

stem: Someone's ears are burning
options: The feeling when someone thinks others are talking about them
Being angry due to unfair criticism
Itching to enter into an argument
explanation: The sensation of suspecting you are being discussed in your absence.

stem: Turn one's stomach
options: Being nauseated by something or someone
Being plagued by a stomach upset
Unable to cope with the changes
explanation: To make one feel sick or disgusted.

stem: The world is your oyster
options: You have all the opportunity to obtain what you wish from life
Life is a puzzle you cannot ever unravel
The world will dazzle you if you are not careful
explanation: You are free to enjoy every opportunity life offers.

stem: Elicit and Illicit
options: Elicit is a verb meaning forbidden by law. Illicit is an adjective meaning draw a reaction from someone.
Elicit is an adverb meaning to draw a reaction from someone. Illicit is a determiner meaning forbidden by law.
Elicit is a verb meaning to draw a reaction from someone. Illicit is an adjective meaning forbidden by law.
Elicit is an adverb meaning forbidden by law. Illicit is an adjective meaning to draw a reaction from someone.
explanation: Elicit (verb) = to draw out (a response); illicit (adjective) = illegal/forbidden.

stem: Ensure and Insure
options: Ensure is a transitive verb meaning to make something certain. Insure is a transitive verb meaning to make certain by taking necessary precaution.
Ensure is a determiner meaning to make something certain. Insure is a determiner meaning to make certain by taking necessary precaution.
Ensure is a determiner meaning to make certain by taking necessary precaution. Insure is a determiner to make something certain.
Ensure is an adjective meaning to make certain by taking necessary precaution. Insure is a conjunction to make something certain.
explanation: Ensure = to guarantee; insure = to protect against loss via insurance. Both are transitive verbs.

stem: Imitated and Intimated
options: Imitate is a pronoun meaning take or follow as a model. Intimate is a noun meaning closely acquainted.
Imitate is an adjective meaning closely acquainted. Intimate is a verb meaning take or follow as a model.
Imitate is an interjection meaning take or follow as a model. Intimate is an adverb meaning closely acquainted.
Imitate is a verb meaning take or follow as a model. Intimate is an adjective meaning closely acquainted.
explanation: Imitate (verb) = to copy; intimate (adjective) = close/familiar.

stem: Compare and Compere
options: Compare is an adjective and it means to estimate or measure something. Compere is a determiner and it refers to the person who introduces the performers.
Compare is a verb and it means to estimate or measure something. Compere is a noun and it refers to the person who introduces the performers.
Compare is a verb and it refers to the person who introduces the performers. Compere is a noun and it means to estimate or measure something.
Compare is a conjunction and it means to estimate or measure something. Compere is an auxiliary verb and it refers to the person who introduces the performers.
explanation: Compare (verb) = to assess similarity; compere (noun) = a master of ceremonies/host.

stem: Precept and Percept
options: Precept is a verb and it means a general rule intended to regulate behaviour. Percept is an adjective and it means an object of perception.
Precept is a pronoun and it means a general rule intended to regulate behaviour. Percept is an adjective and it means an object of perception.
Precept is a noun and it means a general rule intended to regulate behaviour. Percept is a noun and it means an object of perception.

Precept is a noun and it means an object of perception. Percept is a noun and it means a general rule intended to regulate behaviour. Word Usage - only one sentence uses the word correctly (Q96-100)  (5)
explanation: ‘Emigrate’ = to leave one’s own country to settle in another. Animals ‘migrate’ (a), workers moving between provinces are internal ‘migration’ (c), and entry into a country is ‘immigration’ (d).

stem: Lose and Loose
options: Lose is a verb meaning to misplace and loose is an adjective meaning slack or movable.
Lose is an adjective meaning slack or movable and loose is a verb meaning to misplace.
Lose is an adjective meaning to misplace and loose is a verb meaning slack or movable.
Lose is a verb meaning slack or movable and loose is an adjective meaning to misplace.
explanation: Lose (verb) = to be deprived of / misplace; loose (adjective) = not tight, slack.

stem: Anyway and Any way
options: Anyway is an adverb meaning any manner or method. Any way is a phrase meaning regardless.
Anyway is a phrase meaning any manner or method. Any way is an adverb meaning regardless.
Anyway is an adverb meaning regardless. Any way is a phrase meaning any manner or method.
Anyway is a phrase meaning regardless. Any way is an adverb meaning any manner or method.
explanation: ‘Anyway’ (one word) = in any case/regardless; ‘any way’ (two words) = by any method.

stem: Allusion and Illusion
options: Allusion is a noun that is an implied reference to something. Illusion is a noun that refers to a false idea.
Illusion is a noun that is an implied reference to something. Allusion is a noun that refers to a false idea.
Allusion is a verb that refers to an implied reference to something. Illusion is a verb that refers to a false idea.
Illusion is an adverb that refers to an implied reference to something. Allusion is an adjective that refers to a false idea.
explanation: Allusion = an indirect reference; illusion = a false impression or belief.

stem: Appraise and Apprise
options: Appraise is a verb meaning to inform about something. Apprise is a verb meaning to examine and form an opinion about something.
Appraise is a verb meaning to examine and form an opinion about something. Apprise is a verb meaning to inform about something.
Appraise is a noun meaning to inform about something. Apprise is a noun meaning to examine and form an opinion about something.
Appraise is an adverb meaning to examine and form an opinion about something. Apprise is a verb meaning to inform about something.
explanation: To appraise is to evaluate/assess; to apprise is to notify/inform someone.

stem: Dairy, Dreary, Diary  1. She quietly stored all her sacred memories in her private dairy.  2. The proceedings of the meeting went on and on and were quite dreary.  3. They always bought their milk products from the local diary. In which of the sentences given above has / have the word(s) been used correctly?
explanation: 2 ‘proceedings were quite dreary’ ✓ (dull). 1 ‘memories in her private dairy’ ✗ (should be ‘diary’); 3 ‘milk products from the local diary’ ✗ (should be ‘dairy’).

stem: Braid, Beard, Brood  1. The engineers were asked to braid the river by converting it into single body with the aid of an artificial dam.  2. Wise men, they say, often sport a beard.  3. Among certain avian species the males are known to brood the eggs laid by females. In which of the sentences given above has / have the word(s) been used correctly?
explanation: 2 ‘sport a beard’ ✓; 3 ‘males brood the eggs’ ✓ (incubate). 1 ‘braid the river by converting it into a single body’ ✗ — braiding a river means splitting it into many channels, the opposite of a single body.

stem: Lesion, Legion, Lesson  1. The scan of the affected area indicated the presence of a life-threatening lesion.  2. He had a legion of followers who hung on to his every word.  3. It is important to lesson the burden of others as we venture through life. In which of the sentences given above has / have the word(s) been used correctly?
explanation: 1 ‘life-threatening lesion’ ✓ (injury/abnormal tissue); 2 ‘a legion of followers’ ✓ (a great number). 3 ‘to lesson the burden’ ✗ (should be ‘lessen’ = reduce).

stem: Depose, Deplore, Deport  1. The director asked the actor to depose himself and instead act naturally.  2. The people are enthusiastically declaring that they deplore the salutary impact of the decisions taken by the administration.  3. Under extreme circumstances, the government can deport someone from a country. In which of the sentences given above has / have the word(s) been used correctly?
explanation: 3 ‘the government can deport someone’ ✓ (expel from a country). 1 ‘depose himself and act naturally’ ✗ (depose = remove from office / testify); 2 ‘enthusiastically … deplore the salutary impact’ ✗ — deplore = strongly disapprove, which contradicts ‘enthusiastically’ and a ‘salutary (beneficial) impact’.

stem: Bate, Bait, Bade  1. Those who had gathered for the unveiling were asked to bate their curiosity.  2. They set the bait to capture the leopard for tagging.  3. He reluctantly bade goodbye to his belongings. In which of the sentences given above has / have the word(s) been used correctly?
explanation: 2 ‘set the bait to capture the leopard’ ✓; 3 ‘bade goodbye’ ✓ (past tense of bid). 1 ‘asked to bate their curiosity’ ✗ — ‘bate’ (to lessen/restrain) collocates with ‘bated breath’, not ‘curiosity’, so the usage is treated as incorrect.

stem: Someone's ears are flapping
options: Someone in a state of intense agitation
Someone in a state of heightened excitement
Someone desperate to eavesdrop on a conversation
Someone embarrassed at being caught in the act of lying
explanation: Describes someone straining to listen in on others’ talk.

stem: Twist of the knife
options: Slicing through the difficulties that one faces in life
To accept defeat in the face of an enemy
The last thrust of a difficult operation
To make someone who is already feeling bad, feel worse with one's words or actions
explanation: To deliberately worsen another’s pain or difficulty.

stem: Blow your own trumpet
options: Excessively boastful of one's own achievements
Be scathing in one's criticism of others
explanation: To boast about one’s own successes.

stem: Get under someone's skin
options: Fall intensely in love with someone
To cause intense liking in someone
To annoy or irritate someone intensely
explanation: In this sense it means to irritate persistently. (It can also mean to fascinate, but among the options ‘annoy/irritate’ is intended.)

stem: Sweep under the carpet
options: Keep it under wraps in the hope it will be forgotten
Do a quick clean up job
explanation: To conceal something embarrassing or inconvenient and hope it is ignored.

stem: Many hands make light work
options: Many people working together can light a new path
Many people helping can help accomplish a task quickly
Many people working together can make work non-serious
Workers light up the work they undertake
explanation: A shared task is done faster and more easily when many contribute.

stem: Make heavy weather
options: To complain about wet and rainy conditions
Unnecessarily create difficulty in dealing with a task
explanation: To make a task seem more difficult or laborious than it really is.

word: A bird in hand is worth two in the bush
stem: A bird in hand is worth two in the bush
options: What's hidden is always a matter of speculation
What we have is always more valuable than what we have a chance of getting
A gift is always better kept close to oneself
What can happen is always better than what has happened
explanation: A certain, smaller gain is better than a larger but uncertain one.

stem: A purple patch
options: The final bloom of the season
A great run of luck or success or form
A bodily discolouration related to heredity
explanation: A period of notable success or good fortune.

stem: Put out to pasture
explanation: From retiring old animals to graze; to force someone to retire or set them aside as no longer useful.

stem: The opposing political parties nevertheless took a bilateral position in the national interest.
explanation: Two opposing political parties taking a common position = bipartisan. ‘Bilateral’ (between two states/parties) does not fit party politics as neatly.

stem: Although she had sampled food from all the restaurants in the food court, she remained indefinite towards the culinary fare on offer.
explanation: Having sampled all the food, she was unmoved/uninterested toward it = indifferent.

stem: Humanity was rendered diligent due to the crash in the share market.
explanation: A stock-market crash would leave people poor = indigent. ‘Diligent’ (hardworking) makes no sense here.

stem: The document was discerned to be a postscript where clearly an earlier manuscript had been replaced by a new one.
explanation: A manuscript where an earlier text has been erased/overwritten by a new one is a palimpsest.

stem: He was accused of simulism for having copied without due acknowledgement.
explanation: Copying without acknowledgement = plagiarism.

stem: Her actions were considered salutary because they were done without good reason or cause.
explanation: Actions ‘done without good reason or cause’ = gratuitous (uncalled-for). ‘Salutary’ means beneficial, which contradicts the clause.

stem: Pontoon and Platoon
options: Pontoon means a wooden platform and Platoon means a bamboo structure
Pontoon means a group of soldiers and Platoon means a series of bunkers
Pontoon means a temporary bridge and Platoon means a group of soldiers
Pontoon means a group of people and Platoon means a temporary bridge
explanation: A pontoon is a float/flat-bottomed boat used to support a temporary (floating) bridge; a platoon is a small body of soldiers, a sub-unit of a company.

stem: Invoke and Revoke
options: Invoke means to invite trouble for doing something and Revoke means to repay debts
Invoke means to use the law to achieve something and Revoke means to officially cancel permission or agreement
Invoke means to use the law to achieve something and Revoke means to ignore legal procedures
Invoke means to usher newness and Revoke means to stick to tradition
explanation: To invoke is to call upon or cite (a law, right, authority); to revoke is to withdraw or annul something previously granted.

stem: Imminent and Eminent
options: Imminent means immobile and Eminent means extremely large
Imminent means extremely large and Eminent means immobile
Imminent means famous and respected, and Eminent means happening soon
Imminent means happening soon and Eminent means famous and respected
explanation: Imminent = about to occur (impending); eminent = distinguished/prominent. (A third look-alike, ‘immanent’, means inherent.)

stem: Liable and Libel
options: Liable means being legally responsible and Libel means publishing something that is false
Liable means publishing something that is false and Libel means being legally responsible
Liable means the willingness to understand and Libel means being generous
Liable means being generous and Libel means the willingness to understand
explanation: Liable (adj.) = legally answerable; libel (noun/verb) = a published false statement that damages reputation (defamation in permanent form).

stem: Incredible and Incredulous
options: Incredible means impossible to understand something and Incredulous means extremely grateful
Incredible means difficult to believe and Incredulous means not willing to believe
Incredible means not willing to believe and Incredulous means difficult to believe something
Incredible means extremely grateful and Incredulous means impossible to understand something
explanation: Incredible describes the thing (unbelievable); incredulous describes the person (disbelieving/sceptical).

stem: Climatic and Climactic
options: Climatic means related to climate and Climactic means the most exciting part of a story or situation
Climatic means the most exciting part of a story or situation and Climactic means related to climate
Climatic means scientific study of climate and Climactic means climbing to the top of something
Climatic means climbing to the top of something and Climactic means the scientific study of climate
explanation: Climatic derives from ‘climate’; climactic derives from ‘climax’ (the high point).

stem: Cursor and Cursory
options: Cursor means a handwriting type and Cursory means very rude
Cursor means very offensive and Cursory means related to a curse
Cursor means very old norms and Cursory means invoking a curse
Cursor means a pointer and Cursory means something done in haste
explanation: A cursor is the movable indicator on a screen; cursory means hasty and superficial (a cursory glance).

stem: Affect and Effect
options: Affect means the origin of something and Effect means the consequence of an action
Affect means to influence and Effect means the result of an action
Affect means to impact something and Effect means to reason
Affect means the consequence of something and Effect means to influence something
explanation: Affect is normally a verb (to influence); effect is normally a noun (the result). Both fit the sentence best in these roles.

stem: Hard and Hardly
options: Hard means difficult to a great degree and Hardly means scarcely
Hard means difficult and Hardly means very difficult
Hard means with great force and Hardly means to a great degree
Hard means to a great degree and Hardly means never
explanation: ‘Hard’ can be an adjective/adverb meaning difficult or forceful; ‘hardly’ means barely/scarcely — not simply the adverb form of hard.

stem: Conscious and Conscience
options: Conscious means brightness and Conscience means social awareness
Conscious means awareness and Conscience means being spiritually alert
Conscious means being able to perceive and Conscience means inner sense of right and wrong
Conscious means clarity of purpose and Conscience means perception
explanation: Conscious relates to awareness/perception; conscience is the moral faculty distinguishing right from wrong.

word: understatement (‘pandemic done and dusted would be an understatement’)
stem: To say that the pandemic is done and dusted with would be an understatement.
explanation: An understatement deliberately presents something as smaller/less important than it is — i.e., underplaying its significance.

stem: The coach was of the opinion that his erstwhile protege was approaching the nadir of his career.
explanation: Nadir = the lowest point (opposite of zenith/peak).

stem: After the sudden demise of the director of the company, the employees felt stranded.
explanation: To be stranded is to be left helpless/abandoned, exactly like being marooned.

stem: The Professor assured us that her class lectures would be interspersed with practical demonstrations.
explanation: Interspersed = scattered/mixed in among other things; ‘combined’ (with practical demos) is nearest.

stem: She was effusive in her praise.
options: expressing approval while exhibiting strong feelings
explanation: Effusive = gushing, expressing feelings in an unrestrained, wholehearted way.

stem: It is ingenuous to suppose that money did not play a part in his decision.
explanation: Ingenuous = innocent, unsuspecting, naive (not to be confused with ‘ingenious’ = clever).

stem: The advertisements were all posted in a conspicuous place.
explanation: Conspicuous = clearly visible, attracting attention.

stem: Early humans may have had a rudimentary understanding of certain mathematical concepts.
explanation: Rudimentary = basic, undeveloped, elementary.

stem: The poorly conducted workshop was a travesty of the initial vision document.
explanation: A travesty is a grotesque, absurd imitation — a mockery of the original.

stem: The abstruse style of the speaker left the students disinterested and fidgety.
explanation: Abstruse = hard to understand/obscure; recondite is its closest synonym (deep, obscure).

stem: Iconoclast:
options: Person who criticises a cultural icon
Person who attracts a lot of attention
Person who does not adhere to accepted beliefs and traditions
Person who negates his own positions
explanation: Literally ‘image-breaker’; one who attacks or rejects cherished beliefs and established conventions.

stem: Axiom:
options: Quality of being very similar or equal
Rule or principle that most people believe to be true
Rule that says how people should behave
The mathematical principle of being exactly equal
explanation: An axiom is a self-evident, generally accepted truth or principle.

stem: Holistic:
options: Relating to complete systems rather than individual parts
Relating to a particular work in certain industries
explanation: Holistic considers the whole system rather than isolated components.

stem: Obsequious:
options: Excessively confident about oneself in a way that annoys people
Overly eager to help or obey someone important
Far too important and respected to be criticised
explanation: Obsequious = servile, fawning, excessively obedient/attentive to please.

stem: Pachyderm:
explanation: Pachyderm (e.g., elephant, rhino) literally means ‘thick-skinned’.

stem: Ubiquitous:
explanation: Ubiquitous = present everywhere at once; widespread.

stem: Desiccate:
options: To dry out items of consumption
explanation: Desiccate = to dry thoroughly / remove moisture (e.g., desiccated coconut).

stem: Equivocate:
options: To give equal weightage to both arguments
To advocate equally for both sides
To use vague language to conceal the truth
explanation: To equivocate is to be deliberately ambiguous/evasive to mislead or avoid commitment.

stem: Indolent:
explanation: Indolent = habitually idle, averse to activity.

stem: Mealy-mouthed
options: Habituated to inflicting harsh words on others
Lacking braveness to state things forthrightly
explanation: Mealy-mouthed = afraid to speak plainly/directly; evasively polite.

stem: Live in each other's pockets
options: To have a lot of money that is shared with others
To exchange wealth readily with each other
To live by borrowing from each other
To spend too much time together
explanation: Describes two people who are constantly together / excessively close.

stem: Play possum
explanation: From the opossum’s habit of feigning death; to pretend to be dead or asleep/unaware.

stem: Open a can of worms
options: To create suspense about how things will turn out
To spark a situation that creates problems and complications
To create trouble by saying the right thing at the wrong time
To create something without anyone knowing about it
explanation: To start something that leads to many new, unforeseen problems.

stem: More than meets the eye
options: A pleasant feast for the eyes beyond one's imagination
A beautiful spectacle unfolding before one's eyes
The disguised significance of something beyond the obvious
explanation: Means there is a hidden aspect not immediately apparent.

stem: Hold the fort
options: Be present at the precise moment when one is required
Be responsible on behalf of someone who is absent
explanation: To take charge / keep things running while others are away.

stem: It's all Greek to me
options: I can't understand it at all
It's frustrating because it is unfamiliar
explanation: Means something is completely incomprehensible.

stem: To close ranks
options: To stop communicating with one's colleagues
To stop hiring people for new jobs
To unite in order to defend a common interest
explanation: From military drill; to join together solidly, especially to resist criticism/attack.

stem: Be under a cloud
options: To be the object of suspicion
To operate as an undercover agent
explanation: To be under suspicion or in disgrace.

stem: To him everything I did was considered contemptuous.
explanation: Contemptuous = scornful/disdainful; its opposite is reverential (respectful). (Condescending, patronising, arrogant are near-synonyms, not opposites.)

stem: Social media trends are ephemeral.
explanation: Ephemeral = short-lived; opposite is everlasting. (Evanescent, transient, fleeting are synonyms.)

stem: As a person Mohan is generally considered to be parsimonious.
explanation: Parsimonious = stingy/frugal; opposite is extravagant. (Frugal, sparing are synonyms.)

stem: Ramesh has a tempestuous relationship with his neighbours.
explanation: Tempestuous = stormy/turbulent; opposite is tranquil. (Tumultuous, belligerent, riotous are synonyms.)

stem: Arvind had an arduous task to attend to.
explanation: Arduous = difficult and demanding; opposite is easy and effortless.

stem: Malaika has been in a perpetual dilemma since the time she heard the disturbing news.
explanation: Perpetual = never-ending; opposite is transitory (short-lived). (Eternal, continuous, unceasing are synonyms.)

stem: They were distraught on receiving the news.
explanation: Distraught = deeply agitated/upset; opposite is composed/calm. (Flustered, rattled, perturbed are synonyms.)

stem: She is considered to be demure by her family members.
explanation: Demure = modest and reserved; opposite is brazen (bold, shameless). (Sedate, modest, solemn are near-synonyms.)

stem: What he got by way of a settlement was a pittance.
explanation: A pittance is a very small amount; opposite is a bonanza (large windfall). (Petty/miniscule amount are synonyms.)

stem: His academic papers often are viewed as being ponderous.
explanation: Ponderous (of writing/style) = heavy, laboured, dull; opposite is elegant (graceful, fluent).

stem: crops, corps, corpse  1. What are the benefits of growing GM crops?  2. The volunteer corps was organising a blood donation camp.  3. He was sleeping like a corpse. In which of the sentences given above has / have the word(s) been used correctly?
explanation: 1 ‘GM crops’ ✓ (cultivated plants); 2 ‘volunteer corps’ ✓ (an organised group); 3 ‘sleeping like a corpse’ ✓ (motionless as a dead body). All three words are used correctly.

stem: censer, censor, censure  1. The news reports have been heavily censored.  2. The censure board must regulate the cinematic content.  3. Incense was wafting out of the censer carried by the priest. In which of the sentences given above has / have the word(s) been used correctly?
explanation: 1 ‘reports censored’ ✓ (suppress content); 3 ‘incense out of the censer’ ✓ (incense holder); 2 ‘censure board must regulate cinematic content’ ✗ — should be ‘censor board’ (censure = to criticise/blame).

stem: bear, bare, bier  1. She could not bare the extreme heat of Delhi in the month of May.  2. The walls of the store looked bear after the oil paintings had been sold.  3. The body was placed on a bier before the funeral service. In which of the sentences given above has / have the word(s) been used correctly?
explanation: 3 ‘body placed on a bier’ ✓ (funeral stand). 1 ‘could not bare the heat’ ✗ (should be ‘bear’ = endure); 2 ‘walls looked bear’ ✗ (should be ‘bare’ = empty).

stem: tire, tier, tyre  1. The headmaster appealed to the students not to tier the new school building with graffiti.  2. Our guest could not visit us yesterday as his car developed a flat tyre.  3. My uncle lives on the third tire of the building. In which of the sentences given above has / have the word(s) been used correctly?
explanation: 2 ‘car developed a flat tyre’ ✓. 1 ‘tier the building with graffiti’ ✗ (nonsensical; deface would be ‘tar/mar’); 3 ‘third tire of the building’ ✗ (should be ‘tier’ = level).

stem: cite, sight, site  1. The officer has left for her weekly inspection of the cite.  2. The fresh flowers at the desk made for a pleasant sight.  3. Journalists should site their sources accurately. In which of the sentences given above has / have the word(s) been used correctly?
explanation: 2 ‘a pleasant sight’ ✓. 1 ‘inspection of the cite’ ✗ (should be ‘site’ = location); 3 ‘site their sources’ ✗ (should be ‘cite’ = quote).
